metadata.subject: Data Structures (CS1101)
metadata.school: Faculty of Computer Science, Alexandru Ioan Cuza University of Iași
metadata.lesson_reference: Lecture 2: Algorithm efficiency – empirical and asymptotic analysis

curricular_alignment.curriculum_area: Computer Science – Data Structures and Algorithms
general_competencies: Identify and apply fundamental data structures
Design and implement algorithmic solutions in C/pseudocode
Apply abstract data type (ADT) concepts to concrete programming problems
specific_competencies: Apply Example 1. Sum of the ﬁrst n natural numbers
Describe the properties of Asymptotic analysis
key_concepts: Example 1. Sum of the ﬁrst n natural numbers

textbooks_or_references: Mădălina Răschip, Cristian Gațu – CS1101 Data Structures Lecture Notes
T.H. Cormen, C.E. Leiserson, R.L. Rivest, C. Stein – Introduction to Algorithms (CLRS), 3rd ed.

resources.classroom_setup: Lecture hall with projector; students seated in rows
technical_requirements: C compiler available on lab machines

teaching_strategy.instructional_approach: Expository teaching combined with guided discovery through worked examples and exercises
differentiation_strategies: Provide additional simpler examples for struggling students
Pose extension problems for advanced students
Use visual diagrams for abstract concepts

teacher_actions: Introduce lecture topic: Algorithm eﬃciency analysis
Present table of contents: Algorithm eﬃciency analysis, Computation examples, Growth order, Asymptotic notation
Motivate the topic with a real-world application
student_actions: Listen and take notes on the topic overview
questions_or_prompts: What do you already know about Algorithm eﬃciency analysis?
Can you think of a problem where this topic would be useful?
expected_outputs: Students identify the scope and importance of the topic

teacher_actions: Review prerequisite concepts: Lecture 1: algorithms and algorithmic language
Ask quick recall questions about previously studied material
student_actions: Answer recall questions verbally or in writing
Connect previous knowledge to the new topic
questions_or_prompts: Recall: what is the time complexity of a linear search?
What data structure stores elements in LIFO order?
expected_outputs: Bridge to new content is established

Present slides for: Example 1. Sum of the ﬁrst n natural numbers
Present slides for: Growth order
Present slides for: Asymptotic analysis
Explain definitions with examples on the whiteboard

Connect new concepts to previously learned material
student_actions: Take notes and ask clarifying questions
Follow along with pseudocode execution on paper

Pair weak and strong students for peer support

Circulate and observe student work

assessment_methods: Exit ticket: one short question about a key concept
teacher_actions: Pose a quick comprehension question to the whole class
Collect exit ticket responses
Provide immediate corrective feedback on common errors
student_actions: Identify one concept they found difficult
assessment_and_feedback.feedback_strategy: Immediate verbal feedback during class; written feedback on exit tickets returned next session
evidence_of_learning: Successful algorithm tracing during guided practice

teacher_actions: Summarize the key concepts covered
Preview the next lecture topic
Assign homework
student_actions: Write a one-sentence summary of the most important concept
reflection_questions: What is the key insight behind: Execution time?
What is the key insight behind: Example 1. Sum of the ﬁrst n natural numbers?
What is the key insight behind: Growth order?
summary_points: Core concept covered: Example 1. Sum of the ﬁrst n natural numbers
closure.homework_or_follow_up: Implement in C: Problem size: • Assumption: the computing resources volume depends on the input. Complexity: O(n log n) target where applicable.

methods: Exit tickets at end of lecture
criteria: Correctly explains the concept of: Execution time
Correctly explains the concept of: Example 1. Sum of the ﬁrst n natural numbers
Correctly explains the concept of: Growth order
Correctly explains the concept of: Asymptotic analysis
Implements at least one algorithm/structure presented in the lecture
Correctly applies complexity analysis to the discussed examples
Appropriate use of the discussed data structure

prompts: Can I define the key concepts from this lecture without notes?
Can I trace through the algorithm with a new example?
Do I understand why this approach is more efficient than a naïve solution?
Review your partner's algorithm trace — is it correct?
Identify one strength and one improvement in your partner's solution

support_for_struggling_students: Offer additional worked examples with smaller inputs
Allow use of reference sheet during exercises
extensions_for_advanced_students: Analyze a more complex variant of the covered algorithm
Compare the covered approach with an alternative algorithm
Prove or disprove a property discussed in the lecture
accessibility_adaptations: Provide printed slides before the lecture
Use large fonts on the whiteboard
Allow recording of the lecture for later review
differentiation.grouping_strategy: Mixed-ability pairs during guided practice; individual work during independent practice

rules_or_expectations: Raise hand before asking a question
Keep electronic devices on silent unless used for coding
transitions: Signal end of note-taking with a verbal cue and move to exercise mode
Use a 2-minute break between content presentation and guided practice
time_management_notes: Content presentation must not exceed 40 minutes to preserve 50 minutes for practice
If exercises run short, extend with bonus discussion question
Exit ticket should be no longer than 2 minutes
risk_points: Students may struggle with pseudocode notation — allocate extra time for the first few lectures
Complexity analysis can become abstract — always pair with concrete example
classroom_management.backup_plan: If technology fails, deliver lecture using whiteboard only with pre-prepared notes

prompts: What was the most challenging concept in today's lecture?
What strategy did you use to understand the algorithm?
What question do you still have after today's lesson?
student_reflection.collection_method: Written exit ticket or anonymous online form

additional_resources: CLRS Chapter relevant to this lecture
